segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dramatic aerial shot of a flooded village, where murky waters engulf homes and roads, set to an upbeat, slightly dramatic background music. This scene, along with subsequent shots of bustling urban environments like traffic near the Arc de Triomphe in Paris, a crowded crosswalk in a vibrant city, and a stressed woman in a car, is underscored by a male voice stating, "We are living in an era of crises. Worldwide, around 1 billion people are suffering from stress-related illnesses, and that number is rising." The scene then cuts to a man in a dark suit and glasses speaking passionately, who is heard speaking German with an English voice-over explaining that "the key to resilience is staying healthy even when faced with life's great burdens." The narrative transitions to a playful amusement park scene with a child on a ride and a bubble machine, followed by an older man in a white shirt speaking thoughtfully in a dimly lit room with bookshelves. Over these visuals, the male voice asks, "What keeps people mentally healthy in spite of serious crises, while others break down?" The older man in the white shirt is then heard speaking French, with an English voice-over defining resilience as "a natural phenomenon that enables people to continue developing after they experience a catastrophe or traumatic event." The sequence continues with a medical setting, displaying brain scans on a monitor and an EEG technician adjusting a cap on a patient, while the male voice notes, "Leading researchers are trying to identify the secret to resilience." A woman in a black and yellow jacket then speaks directly to the camera, heard speaking German with an English voice-over, stating that "As resilience researchers, we aim to identify strategies that can help prevent people from developing mental illnesses." The final frames show a control room operator monitoring a distorted face, a busy city street with commuters, and a blurred, abstract image of a crowd, as the male voice concludes with the question, "Can we learn to be resilient?" The background music then fades out.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a stylized title sequence for 'RESILIENCE,' featuring a human head silhouette composed of fragmented images, including people, a cage, and birds in flight, symbolizing inner strength and freedom, all set to melancholic, atmospheric instrumental music. The text transitions to 'What Makes the Soul Strong' and credits 'A Report by Kirsten Esch,' against blurred pedestrian feet and flying birds, evoking movement and emotional depth. The scene then shifts to a dimly lit underpass at night, where a man in a dark jacket and jeans walks up a staircase illuminated by a single overhead light. As he emerges onto a train platform during the day, the music continues, punctuated by a train horn, a passing train, and a distant horn. The man, identified as Georg Balmein, stands contemplatively by the railing, looking down the tracks. He speaks in German, translated as, "It was as if part of me had been ripped out. The greatest loss that a father can experience." The quiet platform displays digital signs for Nuremberg and Roth, and a red signal light. Georg leans against the platform wall, arms crossed, gazing thoughtfully into the distance, continuing, "My son, Luca, was killed here on a January evening." A still photograph then appears, showing Georg smiling with Luca at a crowded football stadium, suggesting a cherished memory. The video returns to Georg on the platform, now standing with hands on hips, facing the camera with a serious expression, as if reflecting on his journey or the theme of resilience, adding over a German train station announcement, "That was Saturday morning. Luca's mother Helen called me..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a contemplative shot of Georg Ballmann, a middle-aged man in a dark navy jacket, standing on a quiet, overcast train platform, his serious expression mirroring the somber narrative he begins to share in German, translating to "She just said one thing... Luka is dead." A wider view of the still, isolated Train Platform Scene with multiple tracks and departure boards underscores the setting of the tragedy. As melancholic, bluesy guitar music with a male vocalist singing "Chain 'round your feet" begins, the scene abruptly shifts to vibrant, sunlit images of two shirtless young men, Luka and Freddy, posing confidently in a pool and flexing outdoors in photographs, representing their youthful lives. The narrator explains how, after a birthday party, Luka and his friend Freddy, both 16, got into an argument that escalated, leading to Freddy being pushed in front of an oncoming train, with Luka pulled along. This is visually punctuated by a close-up of a cracked platform edge as a red train passes, followed by a dark nighttime tunnel scene filled with flashing emergency vehicles and firefighters, depicting the tragic aftermath. The music then transitions to a dramatic orchestral piece as the narrative continues, identifying Georg Ballmann as sharing this tragedy with Celine and Björn Wilker, Freddy's parents, as they all grieve for their two sons. The scene moves indoors to a cozy living room where Georg is engaged in conversation with a woman in a red sweater, who appears emotional and reflective. The narrator recounts how in 2019, the police appeared at the Wilkers' door early in the morning. The video concludes with the woman in the red sweater, now alone, speaking with a somber expression, stating in German, "They said we should sit down," reinforcing the profound sense of loss and emotional processing.

He speaks thoughtfully with his hands clasped, reflecting on the aftermath, saying, "The day plays out like a bad movie and you don't realize what's happened." He adds that they were "lucky that friends came to us very quickly and looked after us." The camera zooms in on his serious demeanor. The scene then shifts to a close-up of a photograph on a table, showing a young man in a patterned winter jacket standing in a snowy landscape. This is followed by another photograph of the same young man in a white tank top and cap, making a peace sign against a coastal backdrop, indicating a journey or growth over time. The natural light and domestic interiors maintain an intimate and reflective atmosphere throughout, underscored by the continuing piano music.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a woman in a red sweater, her somber and reflective expression hinting at emotional depth. Her voice, speaking in German and translated to English, reveals her profound grief: "I fully understand people who can't cope. I felt that too. Why do I get to live but my son doesn't? Why do I get to live and why not my son?" The scene transitions to her in a cozy kitchen, where she carefully lights a candle beside vibrant flowers and a framed photograph, a quiet ritual of remembrance. A somber, orchestral music begins, as a male narrator states, "The two families have an endlessly long and painful road ahead." The setting shifts to a bustling subway platform, where a crowd of commuters, including a woman in a red top, boards a moving train, the distinct sounds of which – clanking and movement – blend with the music, capturing the rhythm of urban life. The narrator continues, "Stress and even crises are part of life. Nevertheless, many people stay mentally healthy. The question is, how?" The camera then pans to a wide shot of a river with a barge gliding past a cityscape under an overcast sky, evoking a sense of calm and continuity. The scene cuts to a man in a black coat standing in a busy train station, surrounded by moving escalators and blurred passersby, emphasizing his stillness amidst chaos. Finally, the video settles on neuroscientist and brain researcher Professor Raffael Kalisch, introduced by the narrator, seated in front of a brain scan projection. Kalisch speaks with authority in German, translated to English, asking, "What is mental health? My interest in this question comes from my..." as he gestures thoughtfully, indicating a shift to a scientific or explanatory context. The sequence moves from personal intimacy and grief to public movement and concludes with intellectual discourse, weaving a narrative of human experience across private, urban, and scientific realms.

segments.integrated_caption: Raffael Kalisch, a neuroscientist with short gray hair and glasses, dressed in a black turtleneck and blazer, opens the segment speaking with an authoritative English voiceover, occasionally revealing underlying German speech. He recounts a personal story from his "very early days as a university student," describing how a close friend experienced a "breakdown" while he found everything "fascinating and great and new." Seated expressively with hand gestures in front of a large projection screen displaying a colorful brain scan with highlighted orange and blue neural activity, he explains how watching his friend "fall apart" led him to question, "Why does it happen to some people and not to others?" A brief, contrasting outdoor shot shows a person walking under a bright red umbrella on a wet, overcast day through a modern building complex with gray walls and glass railings, all set to a melancholic instrumental background music featuring piano and strings. The scene then returns to Kalisch in his office, where he sits at a desk with a laptop, a monitor displaying text, and a human skull model, a large window behind him revealing a muted, rainy landscape. Maintaining a serious and engaged demeanor, he continues his explanation, emphasizing that "it's not just really big, extreme life events" like a "serious accident, an act of violence, or the death of a loved one" that can make people mentally ill, but also "minor stresses" if they occur "frequently and over an extended period of time." He concludes his academic discussion back in the initial interview setting, gesturing animatedly against the backdrop of the brain scan.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a close-up of a woman’s hands, her red-painted nails visible as she holds a smartphone displaying a grayscale image of a person on a German-language survey interface titled 'Vorschau des Fragebogens' (Preview of the questionnaire). Her voice, speaking in German with an English translation, describes her personal experience with stress, noting that "home from work was a bit stressful" and "sitting in crowded trains was a bit stressful," but she found relief by making tea and relaxing for ten minutes, ultimately feeling "quite well and and was also very satisfied." The scene then transitions to Raffael Kalisch, a neuroscientist with short gray hair and glasses, seated at a desk in a research office. He gestures toward a large monitor displaying a timeline for the 'MARP RESILIENZ STUDIE' (MARP Resilience Study), explaining in German (with English translation) that by conducting assessments "every three months, we get a very good picture of stress levels over a long period of time," allowing them to observe "how strongly they react psychologically to life's challenges," and ultimately understand "what mental resilience looks like when encountering stressors." He holds a human skull model while explaining the study, emphasizing its scientific nature. As a low, sustained synth background music begins, the video shifts to a clinical setting where the same woman is being prepared for an MRI scan. An MRI technician and a doctor in white coats assist her onto the MRI table, adjusting her position and equipment, including a white earpiece. The large white 'SIEMENS MAGNETOM Prisma' MRI machine is shown as she is moved into the scanner. A male narrator's voice explains that "at regular intervals, participants come to the institute for a thorough examination," and that "Kalisch and his team use MRI machines to look for indications of how mental stress is processed in the brain." The final shot displays a monitor with cross-sectional brain scans, indicating the data being collected during the scan, reinforcing the study’s focus on brain structure and function.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with neuroscientist Raffael Kalisch, wearing a black turtleneck and blazer, speaking with a German accent and expressive hand gestures in front of a large screen displaying a brain scan with highlighted neural activity. He discusses strengthening coping mechanisms, especially for those who struggle. A calm English-speaking narrator then explains that a study in Mainz, nearing completion, has allowed Kalisch to identify resilience factors, including how participants assess their stress levels. Visually, a graph labeled 'Psychische Probleme' (Psychological Problems) illustrates longitudinal mental health data. Kalisch returns, elaborating on the connection between optimism and resilience, stating that those who cultivate a positive assessment style are less likely to be affected. Gentle, contemplative background music begins as the narrator poses the question, 'So, can we influence how resilient we are?' The scene transitions to a woman wearing a face mask, seated on a red couch and working on a laptop, followed by a close-up of a German-language questionnaire featuring self-reporting options like 'Ich habe versucht, die Dinge von einer positiveren Seite zu betrachten' (I tried to look at things from a more positive side). The narrator introduces Professor and psychiatrist Marianne Müller, who is also researching at the Resilience Center in Mainz, investigating what makes some people resilient, as the final scene shifts to a woman standing still in an urban setting under an overpass, contrasting with the moving traffic and pedestrians.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a woman, Marianne Müller, in a black coat, standing under a rainy urban overpass with blurred traffic and cyclists, accompanied by ambient, slightly melancholic background music and a male voice discussing what is 'particularly resilient.' The scene transitions indoors where Marianne Müller, identified by on-screen text as a 'Specialist for Psychiatry and Psychotherapy,' speaks calmly in German, translated by an English voice-over, explaining that this research is promising for better understanding psychiatric illnesses, as previous efforts to understand conditions like stress-related illnesses and clinical depression have had only moderate success. She then walks down a modern, well-lit staircase, transitioning into a sterile laboratory environment. Here, two lab technicians in pink lab gowns, hairnets, and face masks are seen handling transparent cages containing white and black mice. A male narrator explains that while psychiatric research focuses on the clinical picture, the science of resilience is concerned with healthy people, and Müller is exploring the basics of resilient behavior. He further details that with her colleague Ulrich Schmidt, she is investigating this in mice, as resilience can only be measured in the context of stress. This is demonstrated by placing small brown mice in a cage with a much larger and stronger white mouse. The segment concludes with a female voice, translated by an English voice-over, stating, 'We take male mice, which...' as the mice actively move within their enclosures.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Marianne Müller, a Specialist for Psychiatry and Psychotherapy, speaking directly to the camera in a calm, professional setting, with anatomical animal models visible behind her. In a clear, professional tone with a German accent, she explains that male vertebrates, like all or almost all others, exhibit territorial behavior, elaborating that a larger mouse won't accept a test mouse in its home cage, leading to social stress. Calm, slightly melancholic background music then begins as the scene transitions to a sterile animal research lab. Here, Lab Technician 1 and Lab Technician 2, both in pink lab gowns, green hairnets, and face masks, handle transparent plastic cages containing actively moving white and black Laboratory Mice in wood shavings. The technicians carefully transfer a black mouse into a clear cylindrical testing apparatus on a white platform. A male narrator explains that this 'brown mouse' is removed to prevent injury, and the experiment is repeated for 10 days. The camera then shows a computer screen displaying a top-down view of the mouse navigating the apparatus, with its path traced in red by behavioral tracking software. The narrator clarifies the research goal: to understand the long-term effect of this permanent stress on the behavior of the stressed mice. After a day's break, a second experiment is undertaken, where the 'brown mice' are again exposed to a 'white aggressor mouse,' but this time, the white mouse is in a cage. The researchers observe how the previously stressed mice behave, questioning if they stay away fearfully or are brave enough to approach the white aggressor. The scene cuts back to Marianne Müller, who continues her explanation, gesturing with her hands, emphasizing her points, and mentioning 'die Mäuse' (the mice) in German, maintaining the reflective, educational tone amidst the clinical precision of the lab.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a computer screen displaying behavioral tracking software, showing a mouse's movement within a transparent cylindrical apparatus, outlined in orange, while a person in a green hairnet observes. A calm, professional female voice (Marianne Müller), speaking in German, explains that 'the mice were resilient if they always investigated the white mouse a lot, as if they had never experienced stress.' The scene transitions to Marianne Müller, a woman with dark shoulder-length hair in a black top, speaking directly to the camera against a backdrop of anatomical animal models. Upbeat, slightly mysterious background music begins as a top-down view shows a black mouse exploring a white surface near a transparent cylindrical cage, part of a behavioral test setup. Marianne Müller continues, gesturing, her speech translated to English, stating, 'However, we thought this might not be resilience at all, but rather the result of a less than optimal learning process over time. But the test mouse has not learned, has not understood that this white mouse strain is potentially dangerous. If that were the case, this mouse could not be described as resilient.' The video returns to the computer screen, where a hand points to the tracking data. Lab Technician 1 and Lab Technician 2, in pink lab gowns, green hairnets, and face masks, are then seen in the clean, well-lit Animal Research Lab, carefully handling a transparent cylindrical cage containing a mouse, placing it into a larger clear enclosure. A close-up shows a black mouse being released from the cylinder and moving freely within the organized lab environment, complete with shelves of mouse cages and equipment. The background music continues as a male narrator's voice asks, 'So, is the intrepid mouse perhaps just too dumb?' He elaborates that 'The researchers investigate this question in a third experiment,' where 'two large mice are placed behind bars in the cage: The aggressive white one, and a brown one, with which the stressed little mouse has had no negative experiences so far.'

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with gentle, slightly melancholic background music accompanying close-up shots of laboratory mice—first a white, then a black mouse—as they navigate a transparent maze-like testing apparatus with vertical bars and a central rotating component. She elaborates that they were able to show mice can distinguish between a white mouse, which comes from an aggressor strain, and a neutral brown mouse with whom it has had no negative experiences. The scene transitions to a computer monitor in a clean animal research lab, displaying a top-down view of the maze with a red line tracing the mouse’s path, observed by two lab technicians in pink gowns, green hairnets, and face masks, one pointing at the screen. A male voice then asks in English if resilient behavior is about distinguishing between threat and safety. Marianne Müller reappears, continuing in German to define a resilient mouse as one that examines a neutral brown mouse normally but keeps its distance from a white mouse, having learned it's a potential threat, considering this a resilient behavioral phenotype. The video concludes with the black mouse actively moving through the maze, followed by an overhead shot of a white and black mouse interacting in a cage filled with wood shavings, all set to the returning gentle background music, as the male voice begins to elaborate further.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an overhead view of two laboratory mice, one white and one black, moving within a transparent enclosure filled with wood shavings, as a male narrator explains that 'resilient behavior in mice doesn't mean simply bravely confronting every impeding danger, but rather weighing up the situation and adapting behavior accordingly.' The scene transitions to a close-up of a black mouse navigating a clear plastic tube maze, accompanied by gentle, contemplative background music. A scientist in a white lab coat and blue gloves is then seen working with pipettes and lab equipment. Marianne Müller, a Specialist for Psychiatry and Psychotherapy, appears on screen, speaking directly to the camera in a calm, professional manner, with anatomical animal models in the background. She speaks in German, with an English translation overlay, stating that the research is 'directly transferable from mice to humans,' and that 'people who can distinguish between negative stimuli and neutral stimuli are better protected against stress and associated stress-related mental illness.' She continues, expressing confidence that 'we can use this model to derive further neurobiological findings.' The background music becomes more prominent and slightly melancholic as the scene shifts to a serene garden, where a woman in a green jacket with red trim is tending to yellow black-eyed Susans and white roses under bright sunlight. The male narrator returns, identifying her as 'Celine Wilke' and explaining that she is 'finding her way back to life' and 'tries to do this every day after the death of her son,' as the final shot focuses on the delicate white roses.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a somber scene of the Woman in Red Sweater sitting in a warm, domestic living room, her hands clasped, appearing reflective and emotional as she recounts in German, translated as, 'I said goodbye for the last time on Friday evening when he told me cheerfully, 'Mom, I'm off.' I told him to have fun. That was our farewell.' The scene transitions to the Narrating Man, initially seen as the Man on Train Platform, now seated in a similar living room environment, speaking thoughtfully with a serious expression in German, translated as, 'I can still see him in that moment. He was in a good mood, looking forward to the evening with his friends. And in retrospect, I'm glad that the last time we saw each other was a really pleasant moment and that we said goodbye to each other nicely without knowing that we'd never see each other again.' As reflective acoustic guitar music begins, the camera cuts to a framed collage of photographs on a wall, including images of a young man in a football jersey, a group by the sea, and a close-up of a smiling boy, along with a photograph on a wooden table showing the same man and boy lying together in a grassy field near a tent, all indicating cherished memories. The video then shifts to a large outdoor gathering, possibly a memorial, with a crowd standing solemnly around a central figure in white, likely a priest, in a courtyard. The Narrating Man's voice continues in English translation, stating, 'On the day of the funeral, the whole town mourned with the families.' The scene transitions to a cemetery, where the Narrating Man, now in a black jacket, walks past a bronze angel statue, his face etched with grief. Close-ups reveal a gravestone with the name 'LUCA FREDERIK' and birth and death dates, partially covered by ivy, as the Narrating Man adds, 'Luca and Freddy are buried together in the same grave.' The final shot shows the Narrating Man in profile, speaking quietly, his expression heavy with emotion, as he stands near the angel statue, suggesting a moment of personal reflection or tribute, while he concludes, 'And then the whole congregation walked a good kilometer here from the church...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Elisabeth Binder, Director of the Max Planck Institute for Psychiatry, speaking directly to the camera in a dimly lit conference room. Dressed in a black top, she gestures expressively while speaking in German, with an English voice-over translating her discussion on genetic predisposition to stress and the risk of conditions like depression or post-traumatic stress disorder. Subtle electronic background music begins. A male narrator then introduces Elisabeth Binder, explaining her research into whether a gene variant alters stress perception, focusing on the hormone cortisol and its regulation by the HPA axis. During this narration, the scene transitions to a laboratory, showing a gloved hand in green handling a small vial, followed by a white-gloved hand using a pipette to transfer liquid, and then a robotic arm precisely dispensing liquid into a multi-well plate, emphasizing automated research. The video then shifts to an animated sequence, accompanied by subtle ambient sounds, depicting a yellow human silhouette with dark clouds and red lightning, symbolizing mental stress. The narrator explains that stress activates the brain, sending signals to the highlighted hypothalamus, which then releases hormones that travel to the pituitary gland, shown releasing green particles, detailing the stress response pathway.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated diagram illustrating the human brain and adrenal glands, as a male narrator explains how the pituitary gland releases hormones to the adrenal cortex, instructing the body to produce extra cortisol. Green particles are shown traveling from the brain to the labeled adrenal glands, which then release red cortisol particles that spread throughout a yellow human silhouette. The narrator continues, stating that cortisol allows all cells to flood with additional energy to aid in the fight or flight response. The animation transitions to a real-life scene of Elisabeth Binder, identified as Director of Max Planck Institute for Psychiatry, speaking intently at a desk. She explains in German, translated to English, that "Cortisol is our main stress hormone, and it travels to all organs in the body," emphasizing the importance of being well-prepared for a stressful event. The animation returns, showing cortisol affecting the brain, with a toggle switch labeled 'ON' and 'OFF' appearing, symbolizing activation and deactivation. The narrator clarifies that cortisol binds to receptors in cells, reporting back to the brain that enough cortisol is present, thus switching off the stress response and allowing us to calm down, "at least when everything's functioning correctly." Throughout the segment, a subtle electronic background music accompanies the visuals and narration.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Elisabeth Binder, Director of Max Planck Institute for Psychiatry, speaking expressively and gesturing in a dimly lit conference room, as a male voice with a German accent explains that while many people are nervous at interviews, their stress hormone levels should normally down-regulate, but those with a particular genotype struggle, causing their stress hormones to remain high. The scene transitions to a bright, sunlit office where Binder is seated at a desk, focused on a computer with large windows revealing greenery. Gentle, scientific-sounding background music begins as a clear male narrator asks, "The question Binder asks is, why is that? Why are some people not as good at calming down than others? And this is where genetics come into play." The video then shifts to a stylized animation of a human brain, highlighting the hippocampus and amygdala in red, followed by a network of white nodes forming a DNA double helix. The background music becomes more intense and electronic, with a distinct 'pinging' sound effect, as the narrator identifies, "One of the genes responsible for our stress regulation is the FKBP5 gene." The animation labels the 'FKBP5 (gene)' with colored markers, then transitions to a colorful, simplified DNA strand with blue spheres labeled 'FKBP5 (enzyme)' interacting with the helix, illustrating the gene-to-enzyme process. The narrator explains that this gene is "activated during stress and ensures that an important enzyme is released," which "has the same name as its corresponding gene, FKBP5," concluding that "The problem start when too much of it is released," as the electronic music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated sequence, accompanied by subtle, almost ethereal background music, illustrating the biological role of the FKBP5 enzyme as blue spheres interacting with a colorful DNA double helix. A male narrator explains that "the enzyme wedges its way between the stress receptor and the cortisol," as the animation transitions to a green receptor structure where FKBP5 binds, followed by red spheres labeled 'Cortisol' arriving and being marked with a red 'X,' indicating inhibition. This visual is overlaid on a yellow human silhouette with a detailed brain diagram, where red lightning-like lines suggest neural activity or stress, linking cortisol inhibition to brain function. The narrator continues, stating, "The brain is misinformed. It keeps firing, and we can no longer calm down." The scene then shifts to a real-world setting, showing Elisabeth Binder, a woman with dark hair pulled back and wearing a black top, speaking expressively in a dimly lit conference room with a large screen displaying abstract neural imagery behind her. She speaks in German, with an English voiceover explaining, "We think that stress causes too much of this FKBP5 to be released, and that people simply release too much of the stress hormone even after the slightest stress... too much of this stress hormone in the long term is bad for many processes, including the brain, which also increases the risk of psychiatric illnesses." The video cuts to a laboratory environment, underscored by the continuing subtle background music, showcasing automated equipment: a robotic arm dispensing liquid into multi-well plates, labeled with barcodes, and a technician in white gloves handling test tubes with orange liquid, emphasizing high-throughput scientific research. Over these lab scenes, a second male narrator states, "The researchers have identified the FKBP5 gene as one of several important causes of our hormonal stress regulation. Variants in this gene could be partly responsible for why we react with more..." The sequence ends with a close-up of the automated pipetting machine, highlighting precision and scale in modern lab work.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a serene coastal setting, where a man in a blue jacket and beige trousers walks along a stone-lined waterfront path near docked boats, with the sea and distant hills in the background. He approaches a wooden bench, sits down, and appears contemplative, gazing out at the water, accompanied by reflective background music and the sound of waves. A narrator introduces him as psychiatrist and neurologist Boris Cyrulnik, a pioneer of resilience research, whose traumatic childhood gives him a personal perspective. The scene transitions to an indoor interview setting, where Boris Cyrulnik, an older man with glasses and a balding head, wearing a white collared shirt, speaks thoughtfully in French, translated by an English voiceover. He explains that his difficult childhood motivated him to research how people recover after war, having lost almost his entire family in Auschwitz. The narrator adds that as a four-year-old, Boris was left on his own, without parents, hidden by foster families, arrested, and narrowly escaped death. During this narration, the video cuts to black-and-white historical photographs: first, a woman in a suit and hat standing beside a young boy, followed by a close-up of the boy in a formal outfit, visually representing his past. The video returns to Boris Cyrulnik, who continues his interview, gesturing with his hands, recalling how officers said he should be killed, and how he heard adults say he had no family and was lost to life, a thought he refused to accept as a child.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with a blurred, close-up of rippling water and a dark blue garment, accompanied by melancholic piano and strings music. The camera then pans to Boris Cyrulnik, an older man in a light-colored cap and round glasses, wearing a blue jacket, sitting thoughtfully by the water. A narrator's voice explains that at age 11, Cyrulnik already knew he wanted to become a psychiatrist, and after the war, he studied medicine in Paris, supported by the memory of his loving parents. The scene transitions indoors to a warmly lit room filled with bookshelves, where Cyrulnik, now in a white collared shirt, sits reflectively with arms crossed. He speaks in French, translated to English, recalling his determination: "I fought to study, even though everyone told me, 'Don't bother studying, you won't make it.' Well, I made it," concluding with a slight chuckle. The video briefly cuts back to the seaside, showing a man (implied to be Cyrulnik) shirtless on rocks near the water with a dog, before returning to Cyrulnik in his cap by the sea, speaking calmly. He is also seen gesturing subtly while speaking in the indoor setting. The narrator then highlights Cyrulnik's discovery of his life purpose upon encountering the term "resilience." Cyrulnik elaborates, stating, "When I came across the word resilience, I said to myself, this is a word that needs to be developed scientifically... and to make people understand that if we abandon injured people, there will be no resilience." The segment concludes with Cyrulnik seated on a wooden bench by the sea, legs crossed, under a bright, clear sky, as the contemplative music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Boris Cyrulnik, an older man with glasses and a balding head, wearing a light-colored cap and a blue jacket, seated thoughtfully on a wooden bench by a serene coastal landscape of calm blue waters and distant green hills. Gentle, reflective background music begins as a calm English male narrator introduces Cyrulnik's research on child protection, stemming from his own painful experiences, and his particular interest in mother-child interaction. The scene then transitions to a dreamlike, superimposed sequence of a young child in a colorful unicorn-patterned swimsuit playing in the sand, with a woman’s hands gently touching the child’s back in a tender, protective gesture. Boris Cyrulnik, now seen indoors in a white collared shirt against a backdrop of bookshelves, speaks earnestly in French, with an English voice-over, explaining how he started early childhood research in 1981 on the island of Ombie with international researchers. The narrator then poses a key question: what makes one child resistant to crisis while another is vulnerable? The music swells slightly as the narrator explains Cyrulnik sees the mothers of these children as a key factor, describing a vicious cycle where a mother's well-being impacts her child's relationship, potentially beginning during pregnancy. The visual returns to the child at the beach, now being held and comforted by an adult, reinforcing themes of care. Boris concludes by scientifically demonstrating that when a pregnant woman is stressed, stress hormones enter the uterus, his serious and engaged expression emphasizing the depth of his narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with neuropsychiatrist Boris Cyrulnik, an older man with glasses and a balding head, seated in a warmly lit study filled with bookshelves. Speaking thoughtfully in French with a distinct male voice, he gestures expressively as he explains that babies swallow stress hormones like cortisol, which are toxic for their developing brains, leading to cognitive changes caused by the mother's unhappiness rather than the mother herself. Gentle, melancholic background music begins as the scene cuts to a playful beach setting. Here, a young child in a colorful unicorn-patterned swimsuit and a purple cap featuring Disney princesses is seen barefoot, playing in the pebbled sand with a small bucket. An adult woman with her hair in a bun and sunglasses watches from behind, while a shirtless man with sunglasses gently adjusts the child’s cap, showing nurturing care. Over the continuing music, a male narrator, speaking in English, explains that researchers have identified a particularly sensitive phase in the final weeks of pregnancy and the first two years of a child's life, during which new brain networks are constantly forming. The video then returns to Boris Cyrulnik, identified by on-screen text, who continues his explanation in French with expressive hand movements, including pointing to his head, reinforcing the theme of mental processes. He warns that if nobody intervenes, it's a neurological catastrophe where the brain atrophies, but concludes with a hopeful note that intervention is possible. The transitions between Cyrulnik's reflective indoor setting and the nurturing beach scene underscore a thematic link between psychological insight and early childhood experiences.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with neuropsychiatrist Boris Cyrulnik, an older man with glasses and a balding head in a white collared shirt, speaking expressively in a study with bookshelves. He speaks in French, translated, about the importance of intervening gently, very early, and easily to trigger the resilience process, warning that if a child is left isolated for a long time, resilience will be difficult to initiate. As gentle, melancholic instrumental music begins, the scene transitions to a pebbled beach where a family—a shirtless man, a woman in a black bikini, and two young children, one in a colorful unicorn swimsuit with a Disney princess cap—is playing near the water, with children’s beach toys scattered in the foreground. A close-up shows a child’s hand, clad in a floral-patterned long-sleeve swimsuit, picking up pebbles at the water’s edge. The setting then changes to an outdoor institutional area with trees and modern buildings, followed by a laboratory where two women in white lab coats and face masks are working, one adjusting equipment near a machine labeled 'MAGNET 2000'. A computer screen displays a vibrant, colorful neural network image. A male narrator then introduces Elisabeth Binder, Director of Max Planck Institute for Psychiatry, as she appears on screen—a woman with dark hair pulled back, wearing a black top, speaking in a dimly lit room with a large screen behind her showing abstract neural imagery. The narrator explains she is studying this particularly vulnerable phase of life. A male voice, translated from German, elaborates that the biggest correlation is an almost 40-fold increased risk of having attempted suicide when someone experiences severe traumatic experiences as a child. The final scene shows a pregnant woman in a pink top and gray pants, seated with her hands gently resting on her abdomen, as a doctor in a white coat uses a stethoscope to listen to her belly. The narrator concludes that even during pregnancy, too much stress or trauma can lead the embryo's natural barrier against the mother's stress hormones to break. The melancholic music continues softly throughout these transitions.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Elisabeth Binder, Director of Max Planck Institute for Psychiatry, a woman with dark hair pulled back and wearing a black top, speaking expressively in German, her words translated by a male narrator asking, "Why is chronic stress harmful to the brain?" and explaining that "chronic stress leads to our neural circuits becoming weaker," all set against a dimly lit room with abstract neural imagery on a large screen and deep, resonant background music. The scene transitions to a close-up of an ultrasound image showing a developing fetus in warm orange tones with technical data, as the narrator states, "Binder suspects the stress hormone cortisol causes this," and to prove it, "she would have to be able to examine the brain of an embryo under stress." This is followed by a detailed 3D animation of a fetus curled in the womb, connected to the umbilical cord, against a soft amber background with visible blood vessels. The video then cuts back to Elisabeth Binder in a closer shot, continuing her explanation in German, translated as, "It's difficult of course to get to the developing brain and expose it to certain factors and investigate how it reacts," before the narrator interjects, "Because this obviously is not an option." The scene shifts to a laboratory setting where two lab technicians, both wearing white lab coats, face masks, and orange gloves, are working at a bench, one handling petri dishes with red media and using a pipette, while the other works in a biosafety cabinet. The narrator explains that "the researchers are using a novel method to recreate a developing brain outside the womb," and that "a brain-like cell structure is grown from stem cells." The camera focuses on a hand placing a petri dish into a stack, then shows a close-up of a pipette dispensing liquid into a dish. The narrator concludes this part by stating, "The researchers call this simulation a brain organoid." The final shot shows a technician observing a live microscopic view of cells on a large monitor connected to a Leica microscope, displaying magnified, colorful cellular structures in blue and pink hues, as the background music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up, abstract view of a cellular or tissue structure, displaying vibrant purple and pink hues. A male voice, speaking in German with an English translation overlay, explains the limitations and achievements in modeling brain development, stating, "We can only properly model very early brain development... We can't model all cell types in the brain and we can't model how different brain areas talk to each other, so it's a very simple and limited model, but it's the first time we've been able to do this." During this explanation, Elisabeth Binder, identified as the Director of Max Planck Institute for Psychiatry, is seen speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with abstract neural imagery projected behind her. The scene transitions to a laboratory where two women in white lab coats and face masks, Lab Technician 1 and Lab Technician 2, are working. One uses a Leica microscope connected to a monitor displaying a magnified, colorful cellular structure. A gloved hand then uses a multi-channel pipette to transfer liquid into a multi-well plate labeled 'TARA', with a close-up on the pipette’s digital display. Subsequently, the hand uses a pipette with an orange tip to add liquid to a petri dish containing red growth medium. An old-fashioned telephone ringing sound is heard intermittently throughout these lab scenes. A female voice, also speaking German with an English translation overlay, asks, "How old are these organoids now?", to which another voice responds, "These ones are about 40 days old," and a male voice confirms, "40 days old. So that means we could begin..." The female voice concludes, "Yes, this would be a good time to start." The video returns to the microscopic view of the cellular structure, then back to Elisabeth Binder, now seated in a conference room, continuing her animated explanation with the neural imagery still projected behind her. A distinct English male narrator then takes over, stating, "Once the simulated embryonic brain, the brain organoid, has reached a certain stage of maturation, Binder and her team add synthetically produced cortisol." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman in a white lab coat and face mask in a clinical setting, as a male voice introduces "the MAOA gene" as "another stress gene." This transitions to a stylized animation of a DNA double helix, labeled 'MAOA (gene)', against a dark background, accompanied by subtle, ethereal background music. The animation evolves to show the gene transcribing into an enzyme, labeled 'MAOA (enzyme)', with the male voice explaining it provides instructions for "Monoamine Oxidase A." The scene then shifts to an animated representation of a synaptic gap between two neurons, where blue neurotransmitter molecules are released, and colorful emoji-like faces on the neurons change to sad or distressed expressions. The male voice continues, describing how this enzyme migrates to the synaptic clefts, the area between nerve cells, where it acts as a "happiness eater" by degrading "happiness hormones" like serotonin and norepinephrine. The video then cuts to Katharina Domschke, Professor of Psychiatry, speaking in an interview setting with a blurred office background. She speaks in English with a German accent, stating that "one of the main suspects in the development of mental illness is monoamine oxidase A, or MAOA for short," because it "breaks down norepinephrine and serotonin." The background music fades as she speaks. The final shot shows a blurred laboratory with technicians working, as the audio fades out with a final musical note.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a medical professional preparing a patient's arm for blood collection by cleaning the skin with an alcohol wipe, as a male narrator explains that in some individuals, the 'happiness eating MAOA enzyme is more active than in others.' The scene transitions to a close-up of a blood sample being drawn into a labeled vial, emphasizing clinical precision, with the narrator questioning, 'Why is that?' A woman in a white lab coat, possibly a researcher, is then seen handling the blood sample, while the narrator states that 'Domschke takes blood samples to monitor the MAOA gene and see how much methylation has occurred.' This leads into an animated sequence illustrating 'Methylation' on a DNA double helix with a chemical structure diagram appearing to explain the molecular process, which the narrator defines as a chemical process where a methane derivative binds to specific sites on our DNA, deactivating them. The animation then simplifies this concept with a stylized yellow cell containing red structures, one of which is shown 'asleep' with a nightcap and 'Z's, symbolizing gene silencing, which the narrator describes as a 'cap that sits on our genes, putting them to sleep.' He further elaborates that if the cap sits on the gene, it's 'methylated' and 'dormant,' silencing its activity. Animated blue, fuzzy particles—likely representing molecules or cells—then interact with the cell, suggesting biological activity, as the narrator explains that if the cap is removed, it 'becomes active again,' leading to 'more happiness eaters' being produced in the case of the MAOA gene. Finally, Katharina Domschke, a woman in a dark blazer, is shown seated in a modern office with bookshelves and abstract art, speaking expressively with hand gestures, explaining in a clear female voice, first in German and then in English, that 'The MAOA enzyme is more active and breaks down more serotonin and norepinephrine. There's less of those hormones available in the synaptic cleft.'

She continues, stating that positive life events are associated with increased methylation, meaning more 'caps' on the MAOA gene, indicating resilience. Another male voice translates 'resilience status' into German. The scene shifts to Dr. Katharina Domschke in a white lab coat with a name tag, seated at a desk in a clinical office, reviewing documents and writing notes, with a computer monitor and a small plant, as the male English voice concludes that positive environmental influences and experiences affect how our genes are expressed. The scene transitions to a close-up of her speaking thoughtfully, then pulls back to show the trio playing together, before concluding with poignant still images: a photograph of a young man in green sunglasses, and a framed photo of a young man and a woman, placed beside a lit candle and a glass lantern, suggesting a memorial or tribute. segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a serene aerial view of a man in a dark jacket walking two dogs—one white, one black—along a winding dirt path through a grassy, wooded area, accompanied by gentle, melancholic piano music. The visual then shifts to an urban setting, showing a woman in a light gray coat and a black, white, and yellow scarf standing contemplatively on a bridge overlooking a busy street with moving buses and cars, as the music gains a rhythmic, electronic beat. This woman is then identified by the narrator as Clinical Psychologist Michèle Wessa, seen seated in an office with bookshelves, speaking expressively with hand gestures. The narrator states that Wessa, from the Mainz Resilience Center, researches practical help for people in crisis, emphasizing that resilient behavior develops gradually. Michèle Wessa then speaks in German, with a female English voiceover, explaining that it's impossible to develop training courses that make people resilient and efficient in just an hour and a half or a day, clarifying that this is not their goal. As the rhythmic music fades, replaced by a soft, ethereal piano melody, the final visual is a stylized, dark animation of a chained elephant in front of a circus tent, which the narrator links to Wessa's concept of resilience being regulated by the self, hinting at a story she will tell to explain this.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a dark, stylized animation of a brown elephant chained to a post in front of a blurred circus tent, accompanied by gentle, melancholic background music. The scene transitions to Michèle Wessa, a clinical psychologist, speaking directly to the camera in an office setting with bookshelves. Michèle Wessa, with a serious expression, then explains in German (translated by the male voiceover) that the elephant "lacks the belief that it can do it," leading to "learned helplessness." The background music shifts to a more rhythmic, electronic tone as the male voiceover states that "Wessa examines this state in detail in various experiments." The video then shifts to a laboratory setting, showing an EEG Technician observing a live feed of Michèle Wessa being filmed in a blue-lit room, with a monitor displaying a baby’s face and the German word 'unser'. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with clinical psychologist Michèle Wessa, wearing a black blazer and a distinctive black, white, and yellow scarf, speaking directly to the camera in an office setting. Her clear, explanatory female voice introduces an experiment where participants receive an unpleasant noise and a skin stimulus, then are shown circles, triangles, and squares, requiring them to press a different button. The scene transitions to a dimly lit, cool blue-toned laboratory where a participant, wearing a white KN95 mask and headphones, is seated at a computer. We hear the unpleasant, high-pitched noise alongside distinct keyboard and mouse clicks as the participant interacts with the screen, which displays geometric shapes and later a German questionnaire asking 'Wie gestresst fühlen Sie sich durch die Aufgabe?' (How stressed do you feel by the task?) and 'Wie hilflos fühlen Sie sich in Bezug auf die Aufgabe?' (How helpless do you feel regarding the task?). A male voice then takes over the narration, explaining that for one group, pressing the right button stops the noises, while for a second group, it sometimes does and sometimes doesn't. Michèle Wessa reappears, first seated and gesturing as she explains the process, then standing beside a research assistant with short hair and glasses, both observing the participant's screen. Her female voice explains that one group learns control, while the other learns that chance determines what happens. The male voice concludes, emphasizing that if this inconsistency happens repeatedly, it leads to an experience of helplessness, where 'there is nothing we can do.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michèle Wessa, a clinical psychologist, seated in a professional office environment, speaking directly to the camera. Dressed in a black blazer and a distinctive black, white, and yellow scarf, she gestures expressively as her voice, initially in German and then translated, explains how people might recognize losing control from daily life, like a task where the outcome is unpredictable, leading to a feeling of no control, which is a very important aspect of perceived stress. On-screen text identifies her as 'MICHELÈ WESSA, Clinical Psychologist,' with a bookshelf visible in the background. The scene then transitions to a dimly lit laboratory or testing room, accompanied by subtle electronic and ambient background music. A male voiceover begins, asking, "How does the experience of losing control affect future behavior?" A participant, wearing a white KN95 mask and headphones, is shown seated at a computer, interacting with a screen displaying a grid with moving blue dots, sometimes highlighted by a green square. Clicking and electronic beeping sounds, indicative of the experiment, are heard. The male voiceover continues, describing a follow-up experiment where "the two groups can only stop the unpleasant noise by finding the safe green squares on a field as quickly as possible." The camera alternates between close-ups of the participant’s focused eyes and the screen. The voiceover then asks, "Do the two groups behave differently when searching for these squares?" and explains the result: "people who have already felt they were in control in the previous experiment, find these green safe places much faster than the group that experienced a loss of control." Michèle Wessa is later seen standing beside a research assistant, a woman with short hair and glasses, both observing the participant’s screen in the dim, blue-lit room. The clicking and beeping sounds intensify, creating a sense of urgency, as Michèle Wessa's voice begins again in German at the very end of the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with clinical psychologist Michèle Wessa, seated at a desk in a dimly lit laboratory, wearing a black blazer and a distinctive black, white, and yellow scarf. She gestures toward a computer monitor displaying a diagram on 'Perceived Control and Self-Efficacy' while speaking in German, explaining how the experience of past failures leads to passivity. She elaborates on 'the story with the elephant,' describing how one might give up and submit to fate, moving deeper into a cycle of helplessness and passivity. A soft, reflective piano melody begins as a male narrator, speaking in English, states that according to Wessa, freeing oneself from this cycle allows for change in one's life, but questions what about situations that cannot be changed. Michèle Wessa then returns, continuing in German to emphasize that while the actual stressful situation might not be changeable, one can always alter their reaction to it, highlighting the importance of learning to control one's response even if the stressor itself is beyond control. As upbeat, jazzy music starts, the scene transitions to a symbolic animation of a chained brown elephant, which then breaks free, followed by an aerial view of a modern institutional building complex, and finally Michèle Wessa walking outdoors on a cobblestone path amidst autumn leaves, as the narrator concludes that Wessa applies her research findings at a school in Bad Dürkheim, Germany.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an exterior shot of the Werner-Heisenberg-Gymnasium, a modern school building with a gray and red facade, as a student in white walks up the steps toward the entrance. A male narrator begins, explaining that a WHO-sponsored resilience training program is being conducted in a seventh-grade class. The scene transitions to a lively classroom where Michèle Wessa, a clinical psychologist in a black blazer and a distinctive black, white, and yellow scarf, stands at the front, addressing students. A large screen behind her displays the 'BEW·A·RE' logo and 'Herzlich Willkommen!'. The narrator continues, highlighting the significant increase in mental stress among children and young adults, largely due to the COVID pandemic. Michèle Wessa then speaks in German, with a female voiceover translating: "For me, it's important that we give them strategies at an early stage that they can use themselves to protect their mental health." The classroom is engaging, with students seated at desks, some raising hands, others listening attentively or taking notes amidst colorful posters and yellow sticky notes. The narrator adds that Michèle works with students on freeing themselves from stressful spirals of negative thoughts and feelings, posing the question: "What happens when we are convinced that we will fail at something?" Michèle Wessa continues in German, translated as: "Your thoughts influence your actions. If you think you can't do it, then it may well be that you really can't do it." She further elaborates, translated as: "So you would then say yes, I can't do it anyway, so I won't even try," emphasizing the interactive and focused atmosphere of the educational workshop.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a young girl in a maroon sweatshirt, sitting attentively at her desk in a brightly lit classroom, her hands clasped as she listens. The camera pans across a diverse group of students seated at white desks, some with notebooks and pens, others with tablets, all focused on the front. The classroom walls are decorated with vibrant orange bulletin boards covered in yellow sticky notes and German motivational phrases like 'DENKER' (thinker) and 'Wertvoll' (valuable). Michèle Wessa, a clinical psychologist wearing a black blazer and a bright yellow scarf, stands at the front, gesturing expressively as she speaks in German, her calm, clear voice posing a question, translated as: "If negative thoughts lead to bad results, shouldn't the reverse also be possible?" She continues, explaining that this is "precisely where we can do something for our health and resilience, namely by looking more closely at things that we have already achieved." She notes that "we often pay much more attention to the things we haven't achieved... and we somehow forget, even the little things that have gone well." A soft, uplifting piano melody begins as she introduces an exercise, asking students to recall something they did well over the weekend, "even if it's something small." The scene cuts back to the students, showing various reactions: one girl in a white sweater writes notes, while others listen intently or look thoughtful. A boy in a green sweater writes in his notebook, and another student in a black shirt rests his chin on his hand. A young female student, with a clear, slightly hesitant but proud voice, shares her experience in German, translated as: "It was my grandma's birthday last weekend and I baked her a cake... She really liked it, and that made me happy." Michèle Wessa responds encouragingly, "Great, and it made your grandma happy too?", to which the student confirms with a simple "Yeah." The atmosphere is one of active learning, with students participating through listening, writing, and observing in the modern, inviting classroom environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a grand university building, its classical architecture and bustling courtyard filled with students, as a calm male voice questions the extent to which resilience implies social pressure to self-optimize and be ready to perform at all times, following an earlier discussion about coping with crises. The scene transitions to a large lecture hall where students attentively listen, then to close-ups of them taking notes and typing on laptops. A clear, slightly accented female voice begins to explain that resilience simply means finding ways to deal with stress, not necessarily coping with as much as possible, but rather recognizing personal limits and planning time for relaxation to compensate. This explanation continues as the visual shifts to a man eating a salad at his desk in an office, surrounded by multiple monitors, before cutting to the woman in a black top, who is speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures in a dimly lit room with abstract visuals behind her, her voice being the one heard explaining resilience. As instrumental music plays, the final sequence moves to a vibrant, sunlit amusement park where an older man in a cap observes children playing on colorful rides and inflatable structures. A male narrator then returns, introducing resilience researcher Boris Surunick, who also sees government and society as having a duty to create an environment that supports resilience.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a middle-aged man in a light blue button-up shirt, seated in a modern living room with soft lighting, speaking with a serious, reflective expression, his hands clasped. He begins speaking in German, then transitions to English, stating, "A loss like that puts a lot into perspective," and reflecting on how one no longer gets upset about small things, enjoying moments more and moving through life with "much more awareness." As the camera zooms in on his thoughtful gaze, the scene transitions to a couple, a woman with blonde hair in a black dress and a man in a dark jacket, walking out of a building into a sunlit courtyard with a historic red-roofed building in the background. Gentle piano music begins, and a male voice starts singing melodically, "This is my song... And no one can take it away... It's been so long..." The next scene cuts to two shirtless young men in a swimming pool, one wearing a white hat, posing confidently with crossed arms and pointing fingers, exuding youth and energy, while the piano and singing continue. This is followed by a woman with short gray hair and glasses, wearing a red sweater, playing the violin with focused intensity, with sheet music visible in the foreground. Over the continuing music and singing, a male voiceover begins, explaining that "Resilience is not a state of being, but rather a continuous process." The video then shifts to a clinical setting: a woman in a white lab coat and face mask, wearing orange gloves, works at a biosafety cabinet with petri dishes and lab equipment, while a monitor displays a woman’s face in a blue-lit room. The voiceover continues, describing "Our psyche" as "a complex mix of environmental influences, genes, and our own ability to act." The final scene shows an older man with a bald head, wearing a white shirt, writing at a desk by a sunlit window with greenery outside, suggesting a peaceful, contemplative moment. The voiceover concludes, stating, "Resilience is not about happiness. It's about living with all of life's gray areas, surviving crises without losing your mental health," as the piano and singing fade out.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of an older man, bald with round glasses and a light-colored button-up shirt, writing intently in a notebook with a black pen at a wooden table outdoors amidst greenery and dappled sunlight. A male voice, speaking in French with an English translation, begins to reflect on the need "to have a realistic understanding." Upbeat, soulful music with male vocals then starts, singing "This is my song and no one..." The camera slowly tilts up to reveal the man's deeply focused face. The scene transitions indoors to a cozy, book-lined study where the same man gestures thoughtfully as he speaks, his arms crossed or hands moving to emphasize points. His translated voice continues, stating, "Everyone told me, it's not even worth trying. I tried anyway, but that doesn't mean everything went like I wanted. Like everyone else, my resilience is never perfect, never 100%." He adds, "I managed to achieve a lot in spite of everything, but I haven't solved everything. And I think that can be said of you, of me, of everyone." The video cuts back to the outdoor scene, showing the man writing again in the sunlit garden with a brick house and green shutters in the background. The soulful music with vocals resumes, singing "And I wonder if you know what it means to laugh as tears go by." As the music continues, becoming more melancholic and piano-led, overlaid text credits appear, acknowledging the Ballmann and Wilke families, followed by production credits for Kirsten Esch (writer/director), Anton Felixberger (camera), Peter König (editing), and others, concluding with commissioning by SWR and arte, and a 2023 copyright notice. The overall tone blends intimate moments of writing and reflection with formal acknowledgments, underscored by the evolving musical score.
